[[RunningHead]]

Investigating the Impact of Quality of Object Relations and Psychological Mindedness on the Severity of Personality Disorder

Anthony Joyce PHD , Michal Mielimąka MD PHD , David Kealy PHD and John Ogrodniczuk PHD

John Ogrodniczuk

116, 10728 82 Avenue NW, Edmonton, AB T6E 6P5, Canada. Email: ajoyce@ualberta.ca

John Ogrodniczuk  

personality disorder, object relations, psychological mindedness

Investigations of individual differences that contribute to the severity of personality dysfunction are necessary for informing clinical best practices. This study examines the influence of two individual difference variables—quality of object relations (QOR) and psychological mindedness (PM)—on the severity of personality disorder (PD). Specifically, PM was investigated as a moderator of the impact of QOR on PD severity. The sample comprised 138 consecutively admitted outpatients participating in a partial hospitalization evening treatment program, all of whom presented with significant personality dysfunction. QOR emerged as having a significant effect on PD severity, which was significantly moderated by PM. Simple slopes analysis indicated that the association between low QOR (representing more dysfunctional object relations) and PD severity became less pronounced as PM increased from low to high, suggesting a compensatory effect of PM. Implications of the findings for understanding the development of severe PD and clinical practice are considered.

Expressed Emotion and Reflective Functioning Across the Schizotypy Spectrum: Developmental Experiential Factors

Özge Pazar MA , Howard Steele PHD and McWelling Todman PHD

McWelling Todman

80 5th Avenue, 6th Floor, 10011 NY, New York, United States
Email: ozge.pazar@newschool.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7806-9249

Department of Psychology, The New School for Social Research

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1295-654X

McWelling Todman  

expressed emotion, reflective functioning, mentalization, schizotypy, adverse childhood experiences

Expressed Emotion (EE) in relation to caregivers is predictive of relapse and symptom exacerbation over the course of schizophrenia and other severe mental disorders. Even individuals with milder, subclinical manifestations of the schizophrenia genotype, such as schizotypy, may be adversely affected by high levels of EE. A potential source of resilience in high EE caretaking environments might be the capacity for mentalization or reflective functioning (RF), which is the ability to accurately think about the mental states of others and the self. The main hypothesis of the present study is that when mentalization capacity or RF remains intact or is reestablished following childhood adversity, it can act to mitigate the impact of high EE environments. Participants (N = 252) were recruited from an online crowdsourcing site, Mturk. Participants completed a series of measures, including the Multidimensional Schizotypy Scale-Brief (MSS-B), the Level of Expressed Emotion Scale-Past (LEE-P), and one open-ended question from the Adult Attachment Interview (AAI). Results were attained through a series of regression analyses that looked at the relations between EE, Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACE), and schizotypy and general linear models (mediation analyses) that addressed the complex links among EE, ACE, schizotypy, and RF. As predicted by the present study and consistent with the hypothesized protective role of RF, participants with higher levels of overt childhood trauma who recalled higher levels of expressed emotion tended to report fewer schizotypal symptoms if they also maintained high levels of RF. These findings and their implications are discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

“A Sacred Sacrifice?” Exploring Children's Meaning-Making of Separation from Their Parents Due to Political Reasons

Lida Anagnostaki PHD and Alexandra Zaharia MD

Alexandra Zaharia

1 Lampsa Street, Athens 11524, Greece
Email: lanagnostaki@ecd.uoa.gr
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4019-9160

Athens, Greece

Alexandra Zaharia  

sacrifice, ideology, parent-child separation

The present study explored the experiences of the (now adult) offspring of former political prisoners who at a young age were forced to be separated from their parents due to political reasons. During the Greek Civil War and its aftermath, thousands of people were detained in prisons or were sent to concentration camps on remote islands and were thus separated from their families and young children. This particular form of parent-child separation, laden with political and ideological tension, has rarely been studied before from the viewpoint of the children. In the present research, 10 adult men and women who as children were separated from both their parents due to political reasons were interviewed about their current perspectives on their childhood experiences. Their narratives were analyzed using interpretative phenomenological analysis (IPA), a qualitative research method that can offer material suitable for psychodynamic understanding. The analysis of the interviews yielded the overarching theme “sacred sacrifice” and seven subordinate themes, “parental idealization,” “following parental footsteps,” “justification of separation,” “emotional effects of separation,”“political relationship with parents,”“sense of belonging,” and gender differences between “mothers and fathers.” The themes demonstrated that ideology as the perceived etiology of separation defined at large the participants' understanding and meaning-making of their experiences. The participants' defensive maneuvers and self-reflective functioning are also discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

Parental Narcissistic Injury following Child Acquired Brain Injury (ABI): Exploring Self-Object Needs and Emotional Experience

Einat Yehene PHD , Michal Ben-Asher MA , Maya Gerner PHD , Amichai Brezner MD and Jana Landa MD

Jana Landa

Email: einat.yehene@gmail.com
P.O. Box 8401, Tel Aviv-Yaffo 61161, Israel
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-9780-3974

P.O. Box 8401, Tel Aviv-Yaffo 61161, Israel

Ramat Gan, Israel

Jana Landa  

self-psychology, pediatric brain injury, parents, self-object needs, narcissism

This article applies Kohut's theory of self-psychology to parent-child relationship following medical trauma and explores the narcissistic injury among parents of children who undergo redevelopmental trajectory as they recover from pediatric acquired brain injury (pABI). Fifty two parents in the pABI group, and 50 in the control group of typically developing children, participated in this cross-sectional study. Parents' experience was assessed using a multiscale Two-Track Bereavement Questionnaire/Two-Track Coping with Life Questionnaire, respectively, and the Self-object Needs Inventory. Parents in the pABI group reported significantly more emotional hardships and difficulties in coping, a tendency to idealize their past relationship with the child, as well as a significantly lower Need for Twinship and Need for Mirroring. Moderation analysis revealed that—among the pABI group—the higher the Need for Idealization, the few parents reported their relationship with the child preinjury as close and positive. Similarly, the higher their Avoidance of Mirroring, the more conflictual the preinjury relationship with the child was reported. The results highlight that pABI involves a loss and rupture in self-object bonds and enhances parental narcissistic injury. To cope with the loss, parents modify self-object needs and their perception of the preinjury parent-child bond toward estrangement. Such modifications may complicate parent-child relationship postinjury, parental emotional adaptation, and should be addressed in psychotherapeutic work with parents.

The New Sexual Landscape and Contemporary Psychoanalysis, by Danielle Knafo and Rocco Lo Bosco, London: Confer Books, 2020, 216 pp., $16.95 (ISBN 978-1913494186)

Isaac Tylim PsyD

Isaac Tylim

Email: isatyl@gmail.com
128 Wooster Street, New York, NY 10012, United States

Isaac Tylim  

Knafo and Lo Bosco (2017), the coauthors of the award-winning “The Age of Perversion, Desire and Technology in Psychoanalysis and Culture”, have teamed up again for a compelling exploration of contemporary sexuality under a psychoanalytic informed frame. The age of perversion is one where technology has become intrinsically linked to sexuality. Their new book revisits the impact of that link on modern sex culture while offering contemporary practitioners novel ways to explore sex and gender of today's generation. “The New Sexual Landscape” may be viewed as an up-to-date guide to the landscape of what McDougall (1980) has termed neosexualities. The book is a kind of “Lonely Planet” survey in which 212 pages condense a rigorously researched account of sexuality and gender in our digital times. Comprehensive references and a well-organized subject index render this book into a valuable tool for clinicians. This volume may praised not only for the scope of its subject but also for the courage to integrate psychoanalysis with theoretical and empirical data from other social and humanistic disciplines.

Knafo and Lo Bosco (2017) invite the reader to a journey into the mysterious pool of desire manifested by the “human animals” that are part of us all. They attempt successfully—to delve into complex and profound changes in human sexual interactions that have taken place in the last 2 decades. The authors assert that sex, despite today's freedom to experience its different modalities, “continues to be a conundrum, just as it was for previous generations” (p. 63). They add that something about sex eludes explanation, as if it follows a logic of its own.

The authors reflect on sexual trends, viewing them as constantly evolving and transformed. The elusive realm of sex, gender, and identity is eloquently presented and discussed, with special attention to a novel sexual lexicon that is gaining prominence in psychoanalytic narratives. The current sexual era is defined by diversity and fluidity. For the clinician, the message conveyed is clear: one needs to allow exploration and understanding of sexuality outside of the heteronormative framework in order to adequately respond to the needs of a number of potential patients.

The Epistemic Trust Assessment—An Experimental Measure of Epistemic Trust

Paul Schröder-Pfeifer MSc , Anna Georg PHD , Alessandro Talia PHD , Jana Volkert PHD , Beate Ditzen PHD and Svenja Taubner PHD

Svenja Taubner

Bergheimer Street 54, Heidelberg 69115, Germany. Email: Paul.Schroeder-Pfeifer@med.uni-heidelberg.de

Svenja Taubner  

epistemic trust, experimental assessment, communication, trier social stress test, psychotherapy process

Epistemic Trust (ET) describes an individual's trust in the relevance of interpersonally transmitted information. While this concept increasingly informs theories of communication and psychopathology, as well as psychoanalytic change theory, there currently exists no rigorous way of measuring ET. This study describes an experimental paradigm for assessing ET. We designed the ET assessment (ETA) procedure in which we first utilized the Trier Social Stress Test for Groups, which asks participants to engage in public speaking and mental arithmetic in front of two evaluators and other experimental participants. Next, the participants were individually administered a questionnaire, which asked questions about participants' own behavior and overall performance during the interview. Participants were then given a standardized feedback about their behavior and performance, which included information about aspects in which the evaluators were “trustworthy informants” (e.g., participants' objectively measured physiology) and “untrustworthy informants” (e.g., participants' mental states), and they were then asked if they wanted to revise their previous answers. ET was operationalized as the extent to which participants were able to adequately modify their perspective on the basis of evaluators' trustworthy feedback. We controlled for social desirability and personality disorder traits using the Short Scale for Social Desirability (KSE-G) and the short form of the Inventory of Personality Organization (IPO-16). The results confirmed our hypothesis. A majority of participants endorsed trustworthy feedback and rejected untrustworthy feedback. The ETA can be used as an internally validated measure of ET. Future studies validating the ETA in a clinical population are warranted.

Chronic Illness and Disability in Psychoanalysis: A Theoretical Review of Structural Characteristics

Eyal Heled PHD

Eyal Heled

Ramat Gan 52621, Israel. E-mail: eyalheled@gmail.com

Eyal Heled  

chronic illness and disability, rehabilitation psychology, psychoanalysis, acquired disability, congenital disability

Chronic illness and disability (CID) is a broad term associated with a wide range of factors, some of them emotional. Various approaches have addressed the mental reality associated with congenital and acquired disabilities, including the psychoanalytic view. The current article aimed to review the psychoanalytic literature on CID, focusing specifically on its mental features and their implications. Although few authors since Freud have addressed the psychic structure and organization of individuals with CID, there seems to be a consensus regarding shared underlying psychological characteristics with a considerable impact on ego strength and the self. These are accompanied by negative feelings that are often strong and enduring, though not always predictable because of the many factors on which they depend. Review of the relevant literature suggests that psychoanalytic conceptions can benefit CID rehabilitation, potentially providing clinicians with knowledge and tools to improve the well-being of affected persons.

Steven Axelrod

Steven Axelrod  

As a society, we have a man problem. Males as a group under-perform females on many different measures starting early in life—for example, boys are four times more likely than girls to be expelled from preschool, are much more likely to be diagnosed with some form of attention deficit disorder, enter and graduate college at lower rates than their female peers, are more likely to perish from suicide and overdose, and are much more likely to commit homicides and violent acts. Then they die—on average 5 years earlier than the average female. And while a small percentage of men continue to be spectacularly successful in socioeconomic terms, as a group men seem adrift, confused, demoralized, and full of grievance.

Psychoanalysis as a profession has its own man problem and is not well-positioned to help understand and address the overall poor adaptation of males in our society. The fact that the majority of our practitioners are female and treat predominantly female patients can be a barrier to empathizing with the everyday lives of men in our society. Psychoanalysis' internecine battles and all or nothing thinking when it comes to matters of gender (e.g., essentialism vs. postmodern gender theory) make it more difficult for us to effectively address larger issues of male emotional dysfunction and social disaggregation.

In this fraught context, Michael Diamond's book Masculinity and Its Discontents: The Male Psyche and the Inherent Tensions of Maturing Manhood offers a unique and much-needed guide to understanding male psychology. Over more than 30 years, Diamond has built a body of work that is unparalleled for its sustained, careful, and thoughtful exploration of the male psyche. Masculinity and Its Discontents integrates his work on fatherhood, early and midlife male development, gender identity, and the elusiveness of masculinity, into a comprehensive framework for understanding the psychology of men. Diamond's book, sophisticated theoretically and well-grounded in his creative work with male patients, provides both an indispensable guide for the working clinician and an essential foundation for understanding aspects of our contemporary man problem.

Yalom, Strenger, and the Psychodynamics of Inner Freedom: A Contribution to Existential Psychoanalysis

Golan Shahar PHD

Golan Shahar

800 Ben-Gurion Avenues, Beer Sheva 84105, Israel. Email: shaharg@bgu.ac.il

Golan Shahar  

freedom, existential psychoanalysis, Strenger, Yalom

Carlo Strenger was a unique person, for many reasons. One such reason was his ability to integrate the existential (humanistic) and psychoanalytic schools of thought into a seamless whole. Nowhere is this seamless integration more apparent than in Strenger's treatment of inner freedom. In this article, I juxtapose Irvin Yalom's seminal work on the four existential concerns in psychopathology and psychotherapy (death, responsibility, isolation, and meaning) against Strenger's work on the psychodynamics of inner freedom. More specifically, I touch upon Strenger's identification of three psychological processes that enable inner freedom: self-creation (the tendency of some people to create their own personality “from the ashes,” in the face of serious traumatic life circumstances), Sosein (active self-acceptance of one's own mistakes through life), and the act of transcending the fear of insignificance (the ability to live life through one's own eyes rather than through the eyes of historical accounts). Strenger's illumination of these processes, I argue, construes inner freedom as a higher-order existential concern that underlies the four concerns discussed by Yalom. I then discuss the way I put these theoretical observations to use in my theoretical and clinical work, focusing on depression and suicidality.

For One Can Always Start Something New: An Arendtian-Psychoanalytic Reflection on Patient Agency

Emma Acke MSc and Reitske Meganck PHD

Reitske Meganck

Email: Emma.Acke@ugent.be
Henri Dunantlaan 2, 9000 Ghent, Belgium
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6009-3507

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9052-0100

Reitske Meganck  

agency, action, Arendt, psychoanalytic theory, psychotherapy

In this study, we explore the etymology and history of “agency,” link it with relational psychoanalytic literature, and examine how it can be understood within Lacanian psychoanalytic theory and practice. Building on its history, we find that “agency” embodies a binary distinction between the person as an actor, on the one hand, and as a being who is subject to determining structures, on the other. This binary approach, however, limits the debate concerning agency. Arendt's concept of “action,” which builds on the same etymological stem as “agency” (i.e., “agere”), allows for nuance in the concept and moves it beyond its otherwise binary understanding. Through this conceptual work, we draw three conclusions regarding patient agency: (a) agency can only come about in relation to others; (b) initiating a new beginning in speech acts and actions already implies agency; and (c) one must be willing to take responsibility for the consequences of one's actions to be able to feel agentic. Lastly, we relate these three conclusions to Lacanian psychoanalysis, in particular to the notions of alienation and separation, full speech, and guilt.

The Fear of Insignificance From a Socio-Communicative Perspective: Reflections on the Role of Cultural Changes in Carlo Strenger's Thinking

Patrick Luyten PHD , Chloe Campbell PHD and Peter Fonagy PHD

Peter Fonagy

Tiensestraat 102, P.O. Box 3722, 3000 Leuven, Belgium. Email: patrick.luyten@kuleuven.be

Peter Fonagy  

culture, psychoanalysis, mentalizing, epistemic trust

This paper commemorates the work of Carlo Strenger, a prolific writer and unparalleled critic of contemporary culture, with a focus on his thinking concerning the role of cultural changes, and globalization in particular, on the development of what he termed the fear of insignificance. We relate Strenger's thinking in this regard to socioevolutionary and developmental psychopathology approaches concerning the role of culture in engendering a sense of agency and selfhood. These views illustrate our own shift in thinking concerning the role of psychological and sociocultural factors in the development and course of psychopathology. The implications of these views for the role of culture in psychoanalytic theory and practice are discussed.

João Barreto PHD and Paula Matos PHD

Paula Matos

Rua Alfredo Allen, 4200-135, Porto, Portugal

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1893-4819

Paula Matos  

countertransference, mentalization, attachment, alliance, longitudinal multilevel analysis

Individual and combined attachment dimensions within the dyad may affect therapists' experiences in ways that hinder mentalizing and threaten the alliance. Previous research on a particular therapists' mentalizing ability named the elaboration of countertransference experience (ECE) suggests that attachment may affect therapists' mental processes, which in turn are involved in alliance variations between dyads and over time. In this study, these findings are extended by examining therapist, client, and combined attachment dimensions as moderators of the association between ECE and working alliance. Forty-four assessments from the first 10 psychotherapy sessions of 12 independent dyads were examined. The ECE dimension of reflection/conjecture was positively associated with the alliance bond and total score in therapeutic dyads that differed more in attachment. Possible in-session dynamics at stake are discussed.

Heidegger's Nietzsche, the Doctrine of Eternal Return, and the Phenomenology of Human Finitude

Robert Stolorow PHD

Robert Stolorow

851 Galvin Drive, El Cerrito, CA 94530, United States. Email: robertdstolorow@gmail.com

Robert Stolorow  

Nietzsche, Heidegger, eternal return, finitude, temporality

Nietzsche's doctrine of the eternal return of the same, seen through the lens of Heidegger's interpretation, captures the groundlessness of existence in a technological world devoid of normative significance. The author contends that the temporality depicted poetically in the thought of eternal return is the traumatic temporality of human finitude, to which Nietzsche was exposed at the age of 4 when the death of his father shattered his world. Nietzsche's metaphysical position is seen as a metaphorical window into the phenomenology of finitude and of the struggle to overcome it.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two Visions of Psychoanalysis: An Essay in Memory of Carlo Strenger

Morris Eagle PHD ABPP

Morris Eagle

4351 Redwood Avenue, #1, Marina Del Rey, CA 90292, United States. Email: meagle100@aol.com

Morris Eagle  

insight, self-knowledge, subjective experience, false consciousness, affect

Strenger (1989) refers to the classic and romantic vision of psychoanalysis. I build on Strenger's paper and discuss the contrast between the vision of psychoanalysis that emphasizes the enhancement of self-knowledge and that tends to view conscious experience as dissimulating and the vision of psychoanalysis that places its emphasis on the enhancement of the range and richness of subjective experience.

[[RunningHead]]

Lockdown Dreams: Dream Content and Emotions during the COVID-19 Pandemic in an Italian Sample

Guido Giovanardi PHD , Alice Bincoletto PsyD , Roberto Baiocco PHD , Martina Ferrari PsyD , Daniela Gentile PHD , Mauro Siri PsyD , Annalisa Tanzilli PHD and Vittorio Lingiardi MD

Vittorio Lingiardi

Via degli Apuli 1, Rome 00185, Italy. Email: guido.giovanardi@uniroma1.it

Italy

Genoa, Italy

Vittorio Lingiardi  

dreaming, psychoanalysis, COVID-19, lockdown, continuity hypothesis

Beginning in March 2020, the Italian population was subjected to a lockdown lasting approximately 3 months due to the Coronavirus disease (COVID-19) emergency. Drawing on a quantitative and qualitative analysis of dream data, the present study aimed at exploring the effects of social isolation and the lockdown on Italian people's dreams, focusing on content and dominating emotions. An online survey was administered to 598 participants who aged 18-70 years (M = 30.9, SD = 10.9). In the survey, participants were asked to: (a) write down two dreams they had during the lockdown and (b) provide data on demographic and socioeconomic factors; sleep quality before and after lockdown; and levels of anxiety, depression, and rumination. Questions also explored participants’ perceived fear of contagion, dream recall frequency (DRF), and dream vividness. Participants’ dream transcripts revealed a majority of negative emotions—particularly fear/fright/terror and anxiety/anguish/preoccupation. Moreover, several themes emerged from the content analysis, including relationships, the human and natural environment, and COVID-19. The results deepen our understanding of the dominant dream emotions of people experiencing a collective stressful event, such as the social isolation necessitated by the COVID-19 pandemic. Further, the findings support the continuity hypothesis, which holds that dreams tend to reflect the emotional concerns of the dreamer and help to integrate intense or disruptive emotions experienced during waking life.

[[RunningHead]]

Relative Effects of Big Five Traits and Mindset on Malignant Self-Regard

Matthew Yalch PHD and Jessica Sklar MS

Jessica Sklar

1791 Arastradero Road, Palo Alto, CA 94304, United States. Email: myalch@paloaltou.edu

Jessica Sklar  

Big Five, malignant self-regard, mindset, personality disorder, traits

Research is increasingly converging on a dimensional model of personality pathology, although there is not yet broad consensus on the form this model will take. One dimensional model of personality pathology that reflects the rich history of psychodynamic thought is that of malignant self-regard (MSR), a dimension that proponents suggest underlies many forms of personality pathology. Indeed, research suggests that MSR is associated with a number of vulnerable forms of personality pathology, although its link to more basic personality science is less well established. Perhaps the most common and well validated model of basic personality is that of the Big Five traits, which research suggests are strongly associated with numerous forms of personality pathology including MSR. Another aspect of personality independent of Big Five traits is mindset, the belief that one can change one's intrapersonal attributes with effort. Although there is increasing research suggesting the mindset influences personality pathology over and above Big Five traits, there is little research demonstrating this for MSR specifically. In this study, we examined the relative effects of Big Five traits and mindset on MSR using a Bayesian approach to multiple linear regression. We found that Neuroticism is the primary predictor of MSR but mindset about personality (i.e., the belief that one can change one's personality with effort) has an incremental influence over and above Neuroticism and other Big Five traits. These findings contribute to the growing research base on MSR and have implications for future psychodynamically oriented research and clinical intervention.

Object Relations Functioning in a Community Adult Sample: Further Normative Data for the SCORS-G

Liana Galtieri MS , Haley Cobb MS , Emily O'Gorman MS and John Kurtz PHD

John Kurtz

800 Lancaster Avenue, Villanova, PA 19085, United States. Email: john.kurtz@villanova.edu

John Kurtz  

object relations, personality assessment, social cognition

The Social Cognition and Object Relations Scale—Global Rating Method (SCORS-G) examines narrative data on eight dimensions of personality functioning. Research has demonstrated reliability and validity of the SCORS-G, but clinical utility is limited by the lack of data from nonclinical samples to provide a normative basis for interpreation. Bram (2014) introduced data from 63 college students, and the present study sought to extend this normative perspective with data from 140 nonpatient adults from the Eugene-Springfield Community Sample (ESCS). Narrative data obtained using five stimulus pictures from the Picture Story Exercise (PSE; Smith, 1992) demonstrated good to excellent inter-rater reliability across three raters. Median scores for the eight dimensions are generally near the midpoint, and the factor structure replicates that reported by Siefert et al. (2018). Suggestions are offered for establishing formal norms for the SCORS-G.

[[RunningHead]]

Testing the Relationship Between Empirical Paradigms of Repression and Suppression

Anna-Christine Schmidt Dipl.-Psych. , Gerd Waldhauser PHD , Felicitas Hestermann M.Sc. , Aram Kehyayan MD , Henrik Kessler MD and Nikolai Axmacher MD

Nikolai Axmacher

150, 44801 Bochum, Germany
Email: Anna-Christine.Schmidt@rub.de

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0281-8418

Bad Zwesten, Germany
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3381-8760

Ruhr-University Bochum
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1087-5631

Ruhr-University Bochum

Nikolai Axmacher  

defense mechanisms, repression, suppression, memory control, psychoanalytic theory

Forgetting is not always passive but can occur due to active memory control. The psychoanalytic construct of repression was one of the first conceptualizations of motivated forgetting. However, the concept of repression implies various working definitions challenging a standardized empirical approach. A corresponding paradigm, consistent with repression as an unconscious process induced by conflicts and maintained by resistance, is the free association (FA) paradigm. Additionally, the think/no-think paradigm (TNT) has been introduced as a new approach to measure a cognitive aspect of repression, that is, the notion of suppression. It is unknown how memory control in the TNT and the FA paradigms relates to each other. Here, we investigated interindividual correlations between memory control in the TNT and the FA paradigms. We partially replicated previous behavioral results for both paradigms but did not find any association of memory control across the two paradigms, suggesting that they measure different processes. Our results call for a more detailed theoretical elaboration of the nature of the complex phenomenon of repression. It appears that repression is critically driven by emotional and conflict-associated factors that go beyond the pure cognitive process of memory inhibition captured in voluntary suppression.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Toward a Unified Psychoanalytic Theory: Foundation in a Revised and Expanded Ego Psychology, by Morris Eagle, Routledge, 2021, 350 pp., $39.95

Nancy Mcwilliams PHD ABPP

Nancy Mcwilliams

45 Clinton Street, Lambertville, NJ 08530, United States. Email: nancymcw@aol.com

Nancy Mcwilliams  

Morris Eagle's recently published book represents an ambitious undertaking—in fact, I can imagine no project more ambitious than trying to discern and make audible to the rest of us, an underlying, unifying melody in the cacophonies of contemporary psychoanalytic discourse and then making the case for our going forward in some kind of harmony. Eagle joins notable interdisciplinary scholars (e.g., Kandel, 2012; Solms, 2021; the late Jaak Panksepp, Panksepp & Biven, 2012) in viewing psychoanalysis as the most comprehensive theory of human psychology. He renews Freud's effort to create not simply a therapy but a theory of the mind, a project once pursued by ego psychologists such as David Rapaport and Heinz Hartmann.

Eagle is the right person to do this: He is among our community's finest critical thinkers, one of a small number of psychoanalysts for whom the phrase “encyclopedic knowledge” is not hyperbole. He has a firm grasp on the history of psychoanalytic theorizing and has also mastered a wealth of relevant empirical studies. Book-jacket endorsements are in their nature enthusiastic, but in this case, praise comes from readers not noted for gushing. Peter Fonagy hails the book as “a virtuoso achievement,” Glen Gabbard calls it “extraordinary,” Paolo Migone brands it “a classic” (Migone), and Jerome Wakefield finds it “dazzlingly brilliant and breathtaking.” I agree: It is a scholarly tour de force.

Readers familiar with Eagle's previous work will recognize his signature style of framing a critical issue, evaluating the internal coherence of theories germane to the question at hand, considering relevant evidence, dissecting arguments that seem to him facile or inconsistent with the data he has examined and explicated, and drawing reasonable conclusions. The book lays out the basis for the author's convictions that “an expanded and revitalized ego psychology constitutes the strongest foundation for the development of a general psychoanalytic theory of mind,” and that such a project is “capable of integrating the perspectives of different psychoanalytic schools” [italics his] and “needs to integrate findings from nonpsychoanalytic sources” (p. 1). Beginning with Freud, whose ideas he reviews and critiques based on both old and newer research findings (Chapter 1), Eagle systematically explicates the basic tenets of ego psychology (Chapter 2), discusses critiques of it (Chapter 3), and offers empirically based “correctives and revisions” (Chapter 4). Subsequent chapters take up, respectively, interpersonal processes, ego functions, affect, psychopathology, and treatment. In the final chapter, he makes the case for the possibility and desirability of a unified psychoanalytic theory.

Carlo Strenger: Embracing Internal Contradictions

Morris Eagle PHD , Julia Elad-Strenger PHD and Golan Shahar PHD

Golan Shahar

1 South Ave, Garden City, NY 11530, United States. Email: meagle100@aol.com

800 Ben-Gurion Avenues, Beer Sheva 84105, Israel
Email: golan.shahar878@gmail.com

Golan Shahar  

psychoanalysis, existentialism, freedom, Carlo Strenger

Carlo Strenger, PhD, was a person of mysteries, riddles, and contradictions. He lived in Israel, but his heart and mind were really global. Born and raised in Switzerland, he spoke freely all dialects of the European language and culture. Having treated many American patients and having strong professional and personal relationships with people in the USA and Canada, he was intimately familiar with North American culture, science, and psychology. Although not all of those who knew Carlo took a liking to his flamboyant style, his intellectual powers and his contribution to bridging (existential) philosophy, (scientific) psychology, and psychoanalysis were widely acknowledged. In this special section, we honor Strenger's diverse contribution to psychoanalysis. This introduction includes a brief exposition of Carlo's work, followed by a description of six contributions to this special section by authors addressing Strenger's writing.

Attachment Representations and Mentalizing in Middle Childhood Across One Year: A Preliminary Pilot Longitudinal Study

Fabiola Bizzi PHD , Simone Charpentier-Mora PHD , Chiara Bastianoni PHD , Karin Ensink PHD , Donatella Cavanna PHD and Jessica Borelli PHD

Jessica Borelli

C. A. Podestà 2, Genoa 16128, Italy. Email: fabiola.bizzi@unige.it

Irvine

Jessica Borelli  

attachment, mentalizing, child reflective functioning, middle childhood, short-term longitudinal study

This 3-wave short-term longitudinal exploratory pilot study aimed to investigate the stability of both children's attachment (Attachment Narrative Coherence [ANC]) and mentalizing (Reflective Functioning [RF]) and to explore the possible role played by children's mentalizing in promoting attachment security over 1 year. Italian children (N = 22, Mage = 11.30, SD = 2.24) recruited from low-income families in the community completed the Child Attachment Interview at three time points: baseline (Time 1), after 3 months (Time 2), and after 12 months (Time 3). The interviews were coded to assess both ANC and RF. The Verbal Comprehension Index was also assessed. The findings show significant temporal stability of both ANC and RF over 1 year. The cross-lagged regression model revealed that ANC was not a significant predictor of RF at both Time 1 and Time 2; while RF at baseline significantly predicted ANC at Time 2. These findings suggest that children's capacity to mentalize and think about themselves and their relationships with attachment figures in mental state terms facilitates their ANC, an indicator associated with attachment security.

psychodynamic intervention competencies, psychotherapy training, role-play, standardized patients, feedback

In psychotherapy training, longitudinal practice-based curricula for the development of psychodynamic intervention competencies are scarce. Based on Kern et al.'s (1998) cycle of curriculum development we conceptualized, implemented and evaluated a longitudinal curriculum for the development of psychodynamic intervention competencies (DYNAMIC curriculum). Didactic elements include practice-oriented training in small groups, enfolding model learning, peer- and standardized patient role-play, and qualified feedback. To qualitatively explore (a) perceived competency development of psychotherapy trainees participating in the DYNAMIC curriculum, and (b) their evaluation of the didactic formats. The curriculum comprises eight modules addressing specific psychodynamic intervention types and was implemented at a university-based German psychotherapy training institute. For qualitative evaluation, semistandardized interviews were conducted with trainees after each module. Questions addressed (a) self-perceived competency development, and (b) evaluation of teaching formats. Interviews were analyzed applying qualitative content analysis (Mayring, 2015). Eighty-six interviews with 41 participants were conducted. (a) Participants perceived a growth in their psychodynamic intervention competencies with respect to skills, attitude, and knowledge (1) using the interventions more deliberately, (2) adapted to patients'-and situational conditions, (3) feeling more self-confident. They realized the importance of the therapeutic relationship and the trade-off between activity and reluctance in their therapeutic role. (b) Interviewees evaluated the role-plays plus feedback as a valuable opportunity to practice the interventions in a protected fault-forgiving environment. Participants perceived the DYNAMIC curriculum as increasing their psychodynamic intervention competencies and shaping their therapeutic attitude.

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Vulnerability: Impact on Theory and Practice by Karen J. Maroda, London: Routledge, 2022, 215 pp.

William Cornell MA

William Cornell

Email: wfcornell@gmail.com
145 44th Street, Pittsburgh, PA 15201, United States
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5817-6338

William Cornell  

The Analyst's Vulnerability is Karen Maroda's fourth book since the publication just over 30 years ago of her first, ground-breaking volume, The Power of Countertransference. In her latest undertaking, Maroda places the humanness of the psychoanalyst at the center of her attention and steps very directly into several of the current controversies in psychoanalytic theory and technique. She reconceptualizes the place of conflict in the psychoanalytic process and scrutinizes several of the assumptions and biases of contemporary relational models.

One of the singular qualities of Maroda's writing (1991, 1999, 2010) has been her ability to write as a psychoanalyst while bridging the too-common gap that has separated much of analytic writing from the broader psychotherapeutic community. Central to her capacity to build these bridges has been her willingness to speak directly to the lived experiences of both analyst/therapist and patient, experiences that are not always so well accounted for in analytic theories. Her first book challenged that long-held belief in analytic authority and abstinence, having from her own experience “felt stalemated, both as patient and therapist, … plagued by feelings of restraint and artificiality” (1991, p. 1). She later described how she felt during her first tentative challenges to psychoanalytic dogma, “I carried out my experiments with sweaty palms and a queasy stomach, but found they paid off when therapeutic impasses were broken” (2010, pp. 2-3). In the preparation for her Psychodynamic Techniques (2010), Maroda had a study group of experienced and inexperienced psychotherapists read and discuss each chapter with her as they were being written. In this latest book, Maroda acknowledges that many of her current patients are themselves psychotherapists and psychoanalysts, and once again, their lived experiences in both personal and professional lives seem to have provided much of the ground for her explorations here.

The Analyst's Vulnerability is divided into two subsections, “The analyst as a person” and “The analyst as a clinician.” The first section is devoted to an examination of the analyst's conscious and unconscious motivations, vulnerabilities, beliefs, anxieties, and gratifications that shape and warp the psychotherapeutic processes and relationships. Maroda argues that the therapist's identity and motivations carry the deep stamp of early life experiences. While this, in and of itself, would not come as a surprise to psychodynamic practitioners, Maroda places particular emphasis on the enduring consequences of psychoanalysts as caretakers in their family system experiences that predispose them to the provision of comfort and avoidance of conflict, as well as a compelling ambivalence toward their own needs and wounds.

Steve Tuber PHD ABPP

Steve Tuber

Email: Steve.tuber@gmail.com
310 West End Avenue, 1B, New York, NY 10023, United States

Steve Tuber  

Given the opportunity to review this replacement and annotated version of Rorschach's Psychodiagnostics is both a blessing and a curse. As someone who has been enthralled by the heuristic value of the Rorschach inkblot method (RIM) since I began graduate school in 1976, I relished the chance to speak to this wellspring of nuanced thought about the task and its utility. I was so fortunate to be trained by such giants in our field as Irene Fast, Tom Cummings, Sid Blatt, and especially Marty Mayman that I have long felt part of the generational links from David Rapaport to the others I just listed and on to my nearly 500 doctoral students. This is indeed the blessed aspect of being asked to review the work that spawned Rapaport's initial interest in the task. Having also spent the past 39 years and counting teaching the Rorschach, first at what was then called Roosevelt Hospital in New York and then for the past 37 years at City College's Doctoral Program in Clinical Psychology, I could not help but read the work from the point of view of a student first learning about the RIM. This would indeed be the “curse” of providing this review. Would I be able to deem the book to be a valuable teaching device, especially in the context of teaching the RIM through a psychodynamic lens, as I have done all this time? I was first trained to score the RIM using the approach developed by Klopfer et al. (1954) with form level scoring by Mayman's (unpublished, 1971) method and then later via the thought disorder system developed by Blatt and Ritzler (1974) and the Mutuality of Autonomy scale developed by Urist (1977). Would Rorschach's opus be a suitable replacement or addition for my students in their development as Rorschach assessors, thus became the central question I needed to stress as I reviewed the work.

Regrettably, although the book has many wonderful virtues, I do not think it fills the bill as the prime text from which to teach the Rorschach to beginners. The book is a remarkably astute collection of truly refined thinking, admirably based on Rorschach's efforts to provide empirical data for all of his hypotheses about what the nature of RIM responses represent. It was the culmination of a decade's worth of work with his “experiment” and the book now includes two articles given just prior to its original publication that reveal how his thinking evolved in regard to the task's theoretical bases. Its linking of the task to psychodynamic approaches to understanding personality cemented the measure's idiographic usefulness for the past 100 years. The degree to which it captures many of the major scoring categories we use today is nothing short of remarkable and thus it is easy to rue his early passing at age 38 from a ruptured appendix. He truly would have been a giant in the field, above and beyond his eponymous “experiment.” The authors of this translated version also provide a number of (I wish there were more!) examples of his personal qualities, suggesting his kindness, integrity, and devotion to his patients that make him still more appealing as a powerful yet beloved emerging leader in the field. Yet, despite the many improvements in making the language of the original translation of 1942 clearer and more experience-near, Psychodiagnostics is simply too technical and data heavy a work to readily permit entry-level students to take to the method. Because the RIM is so multidimensional, it takes a long time to master (as Rorschach himself noted) and thus its initial text, its initial learning tool, must be more directly integrative of how theory begets the process of administration and both then beget scoring. Psychodiagnostics simply does not do that well enough. At the same time, however, there are wonderful if brief clinical vignettes he casually mentions in a variety of contexts and a total of 28 brief case examples that are so spot on and so straightforward, that we must lament that his all too brief life prevented us from enjoying the fruits of his remarkable clinical acumen.

[[RunningHead]]

The Hidden Spring: A Journey to the Source of Consciousness by Mark Solms, W.W. Norton, 2021, 432 pp., $28.95

Joseph Ledoux PHD

Joseph Ledoux

New York, NY 10003, United States. Email: jel1@nyu.edu

Joseph Ledoux  

Emotions are perhaps the most important topic to understand in relation to the brain. Not just because they are so personal, so self-involving, but also because they are the source of much human suffering. The World Health Organization has said that mental health is currently a global crisis, and emotional disorders are a central part of the problem.

This is not due to a lack of research on emotion. Emotions have been extensively studied, both psychologically and neurobiologically. But the question is, what have we learned? The answer depends on a far deeper question. What is an emotion? Until we answer that question, knowing what we have learned will remain out of reach, since we have to know what we are looking for in order to find it.

One answer was provided in the 19th century by Darwin, according to whom, emotions are mental states that we have inherited from our animal ancestors by virtue of having inherited some feature of their nervous system. He in fact often described animal behavior in human emotional terms: cheerful, proud, scornful, content, jealous, contemptuous. A reporter once asked him why he talked this way: He replied that it was kinder, and that the public was more likely to accept animals being like humans than humans being like animals. Another kind of answer came from Williams James, who argued that emotions are conscious experiences that result when the signals from body responses feed back to the brain. A third view is that emotions are cognitive interpretations of situations, which in some ways also goes back to James. All three positions have active adherents today.

Mark Solms' book, The Hidden Spring, is squarely in the Darwinian tradition with regards to emotions, but with a very strong Freudian slant to both the topic, and to the history of psychology. Solms is an excellent writer, and his book tells an engaging story about his scientific career. Before reading it, I was aware of his general approach to the mind but enjoyed learning more about his justification for his views and, in general, about his scientific story. Though I disagree with the thrust of some of his conclusions, I enjoyed the book as a reader.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mania in the Mirror: A Qualitative Study of Recovery of Mania within a Lacanian Psychoanalytic Framework

Bart Rabaey MSCP and Stijn Vanheule PHD

Stijn Vanheule

Henri Dunantlaan 2, 9000 Ghent, Belgium
Email: bart.rabaey@ugent.be
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-7452-4777

Stijn Vanheule  

mania, identity, bipolar disorder, Lacan, psychoanalysis

The aim of this study is to contribute to the understanding of recovery from and dealing with experiences of mania within a Lacanian psychoanalytic framework and to build on existing Lacanian theory to clarify processes of identification involved in recovering from experiences of mania. Based on a qualitative interview study including 18 individuals with experiences of mania, the authors undertook a thematic analysis of the interview transcripts, resulting in six themes that form a trajectory of identity construction. The first part of the results provides a thematic description of the steps that participants took in regard to living with their manic experiences. Subsequently, the authors interpret the results of the initial bottom-up thematic analysis using Lacan's model of the double mirror, a theoretical model of identity and subjectivity concerning the relationship between drives, drive-regulation, and identification. In terms of Lacan's model, the authors describe the trajectory of recovery as one of gaining mastery over manic experiences by developing ways of representing the drive and by adopting and further developing a shared narrative about the drive.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Only Chance and Circumstances? Or, How Freudian are Freudian Slips? A Review of Research Literature Concerning Parapraxes

Gerald Poscheschnik Mag. Dr. and Gianluca Crepaldi MMag.phil. Dr.phil.

Gianluca Crepaldi

Campus Feldkirchen, Hauptplatz 12, A-9560 Feldkirchen, Kärnten, Austria
Email: g.poscheschnik@fh-kaernten.at

Gianluca Crepaldi  

parapraxis, Freudian slip, error, mishap, empirical research

Unlike most psychoanalytic concepts, parapraxis has seen little systematic debate since first being propounded 120 years ago in The Psychopathology of Everyday Life. In this article, the authors first outline the psychoanalytic understanding of human error, beginning with Freud's view of parapraxes as being determined by repression, regulation of unpleasure, unconscious conflicts, associative links, and a series of conducive and inhibiting conditions. Then the authors debate whether this psychoanalytic understanding of errors can withstand empirical investigation. A review of the evidence from experimental psychology, accident research, and psychotherapy research seems to support psychodynamic hypotheses to some extent.

Empathy and the Historical Understanding of the Human Past, by Thomas A. Kohut. Routledge, 2020. 166 pp., $31.96 (ISBN 9780367425784).

James Anderson PHD

James Anderson

Email: j-anderson3@northwestern.edu
122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1300, Chicago, IL 60603, United States
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2447-5791

James Anderson  

Sigmund Freud called the interpretation of dreams “the royal road to a knowledge of the unconscious activities of the mind” (Freud, 1953/1900, p. 608). Some psychoanalysts today might agree with Freud's statement, and personally I value dream interpretation, but that practice is no longer so prioritized. Many psychoanalysts would declare empathy to be the royal road to the unconscious. But there is much confusion and puzzlement about just what empathy is.

Thomas A. Kohut has written a book that he describes as a primer on empathy for historians. I see it as the definitive exploration of empathy. Though written with historians in mind, it is of equal value for the large majority of readers of this journal who are psychoanalysts and psychoanalytic therapists. With those readers in mind, I will emphasize ways in which the book is relevant to them.

Kohut, a distinguished historian specializing in the study of modern Germany, is a professor at Williams College. His writings include a psychologically informed biography of Kaiser Wilhelm II (Kohut, 1991). In addition to having a doctorate in history from the University of Minnesota, he underwent training as a research candidate at the Cincinnati Psychoanalytic Institute. Few readers of this review have failed to note by now that he has the same last name as did the influential psychoanalyst Heinz Kohut, who, indeed, was his father. Kohut (2020, p. xi) notes that his father saw empathy as defining “psychoanalysis as a field of inquiry into the inner life of human beings.” Thomas Kohut is essentially bringing his father's view to historians. Those scholars inevitably use empathy, he notes, but do so with discomfort and awkwardness because they tend to believe that the writing of history should be rational and objective. He sets out to explain what empathy is and how and why to use it.

Kohut (2020, p. 4) describes empathy as a way to know people “by imagining, thinking, and perhaps even feeling one's way inside their experience.” The key distinction he makes throughout the book is between the empathic and the external observational positions. For historians, the external position refers to a scholar viewing the past “from his or her own perspective, with all the benefit of hindsight, distance, and scope” (Kohut, 2020, p. 4). “The historian occupying the internal or empathic position,” Kohut (2020, p. 4) notes, “seeks to view historical phenomena from the perspective of the people of the past, to view and understand their world as they themselves viewed and understood it.” According to Kohut, both approaches are necessary, but many historians ignore the empathic approach or execute it poorly, in part because they have received no training in that approach.

The Analyst's Desire: The Ethical Foundation of Clinical Practice, by Mitchell Wilson, Bloomsbury Academic, 2020, 223 pp., $110

David Lichtenstein PHD

David Lichtenstein

New York, NY 10128, United States. Email: dlichtenstein@gmail.com

David Lichtenstein  

The transference is a phenomenon in which subject and psycho-analyst are both included. To divide it in terms of transference and countertransference—however bold, however confident what is said on this them may be—is never more than a way of avoiding the essence of the matter. (Lacan, 1964/1981, Seminar XI, p. 231)

Mitchell Wilson is a writer, which is not so for all psychoanalysts who write. His care for the grace and rhythm of the voice, for the music of the prose, expresses a desire not only to convey his argument but to evoke desire in the reader. The desire to affect the desire of the reader arises implicitly—structurally—whenever one puts pen to paper. Even the most dry and scientific writing presumes a desire to know in the reader. Wilson's writing is not dry and scientific. It is lively writing. The reader is aroused by the play of language not only by the presentation of ideas, not only by the desire to know but the desire to know the other, the author. The presence of the authorial voice and care for its effects on the other are essential elements of this important and evocative book as is the awareness and responsibility inherent in arousing the desire of the other.

How the writer seeks to arouse desire in the reader is a topic parallel to Wilson's main topic: How the speech of the analyst is implicated in the interplay of desire in psychoanalytic treatment. Just as there are differences in writing that affect the mode of reading, there are differences in speech that affect both listening and hearing and the relationship between the two. This has profound implications for the ethical responsibility of analytic praxis.

That the analyst is responsible for, and should be cognizant of, how the analytic discourse is affecting the analysand and especially how the interest of the analyst arouses desire in the analysand has of course been noted since Freud's “Observations on Transference-Love” (1915). Wilson's work might be seen as an extended reflection on this responsibility. He offers his own new synthesis on this topic and situates it in relation to many analytic thinkers. The interplay of speech and desire is being used as a lens to view psychoanalytic praxis and Wilson successfully shows that by doing so, many important insights emerge regarding core principles of treatment as well as longstanding theoretical debates.

[[RunningHead]]

Interpersonal Sensitivities Associated with Sexual Assault

Kayleigh Watters MA MS and Matthew Yalch PHD

Matthew Yalch

1791 Arastradero Road, Palo Alto, CA 94304, United States
Email: kwatters@paloaltou.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9458-1840

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1965-0041

Matthew Yalch  

sexual assault, interpersonal circumplex, interpersonal sensitivities

Sexual assault is a common traumatic stressor associated with a number of psychosocial problems for adult men and women. The problems associated with sexual assault are thought to be attributable to its complexity as an interpersonal behavior. However, the interpersonal ramifications of sexual assault are unclear. One way of understanding the nature of interpersonal behavior is by using a tool that has deep roots in psychoanalytic thought, the interpersonal circumplex (IPC). The IPC is a graphical tool used to map the interpersonal salience of behavior across two dimensions, warmth (vs. coldness) and dominance (vs. submissiveness). In this study, we examined the interpersonal sensitivities related to sexual assault in a sample of adult men and women (N = 390) using a bootstrapped structural summary approach to mapping constructs across the IPC. Results indicated that sexual assault is associated with a sensitivity to submissiveness. Results further suggested some differences in how sexual assault is differentially associated with interpersonal sensitivity for men and women. Men who experienced sexual assault are especially sensitive to submissiveness, while women who experienced sexual assault are sensitive to submissiveness as well as many other kinds of interpersonal behaviors. These findings suggest directions for future research and have implications for working clinically with adult survivors of sexual assault.

Home in Border Crossings: A Psychoanalytic Study of Dislocations in the Lives of Female Domestic Workers

Ekta Srivastava MA

Ekta Srivastava

Email: ektasrivastava1310@gmail.com
Lothian Road, Kashmere Gate, Delhi 110006, India

Ekta Srivastava   Completed her MA in psychosocial clinical studies from the School of Human Studies, Ambedkar University Delhi, and is currently a doctoral candidate at the Department of Psychosocial and Psychoanalytic Studies, University of Essex. Her present research seeks to explore the relational dialectics of home and dislocation in the lives of migrant women residing in urban villages in New Delhi. Her interests lie chiefly in critical psychoanalysis, cultural psychology, phenomenology, and explorations of and through art and language. She is also committed to psychosocial therapeutic engagement and has been involved with support work at a refugee camp for several months.
dislocation, home, psychoanalysis, gender, domestic work

Traversing psychoanalytic inroads into the lived experiences of dislocations explored with three female domestic workers in India, this research article attempts to excavate the relational contours of “home” in their lives—from the shadows that multiple home losses left behind. Examining home from sociocultural, interpersonal and intrapsychic frames, with a longitudinal view of “degrees of homeness”1 as encountered in different phases of life, it also brings to light insidious forms of dislocation culturally engraved into gendered bodies and psyches—and proposes an understanding of how these complex states of dislocatedness influence the way in which later geographical dislocation and its corresponding work of home-building or recovery are experienced and navigated. Developed out of 17 unstructured and exploratory interviews, this work hopes to open up new approaches to the psychoanalytic study of social and material realities, while offering an analysis of the experience of inhabiting one such nonunitary reality—occupied by female domestic workers in the city.

[[RunningHead]]

On Wilfred R. Bion's Way of Being: Linking Truth, Thought, and Nostalgia

Vineet Gairola MA

Vineet Gairola

House No. 702, KM-64, Jaypee Kosmos, Noida Sector 134, Noida, UP 201304, India. Email: vineetworkz@gmail.com

Vineet Gairola   Currently a doctoral student at the Indian Institute of Technology, Hyderabad (IIT Hyderabad). His doctoral dissertation examines the spiritual practices of the Uttarakhand Himalayas. He has been Adjunct Faculty of Psychology at Ambedkar University, Delhi, and a Research Assistant in the Department of Educational Psychology and Foundations of Education, NCERT, New Delhi. He is UGC-NET JRF qualified in Psychology. His research interests focus on cultural psychology, music psychology, and the correspondence between spirituality, psychopathology, and analytical psychology. He is a regular participant as a researcher in conferences organized across India and abroad.
Wilfred R, Bion, thought, truth, psychoanalysis, mysticism

Wilfred R. Bion's clinical thinking was profoundly impacted by his early childhood experiences in India. In this article, the author elucidates Bion's perspective on how “thought” is different from “thinking.” Bion's notion of truth and thought takes us from chronological to the timeless. His longing to return to India is also evident in his theorization of “O.” The clinical relevance of Bion's ideas is much debated and discussed, but rarely has any psychoanalytic writing touched upon the way in which psychoanalysis is linked with the sonic and sacred aspects of Om. Exploring the truth of and the truth in Bionian psychoanalytic thought will take us into the unexplored paths of how the experience of culture meets experience in the clinic. A sincere and critical reflection of the ideas proposed by Bion would certainly help us to locate its unexplored referents. He never came back to India, but his theories and ideas attempted to create contact with his early childhood experiences. Bion left India, but India never left him. Bion was a Hindu boy belonging to a Christian body. The ways in which Bion conceptualizes neurosis and psychosis are elucidated in this study. However, some of his ideas remain a challenge, fathoming which, requires remembering, repeating, and working through the clinical and mystical elements in his writing.

States of Mind Preceding a Near-Lethal Suicide Attempt: A Mixed Methods Study

Jane Tillman PHD , Jennifer Stevens PHD and Katie Lewis PHD

Katie Lewis

25 Main Street, Stockbridge, MA 01262, United States
Email: Jane.Tillman@austenriggs.net

Stockbridge, Massachusetts, United States

Katie Lewis  

suicide attempt, interpretative phenomenological analysis, developmental psychopathology, suicidal thoughts and behavior

Near-lethal suicide (NLS) attempt survivors are the nearest surrogates we have for completed suicide. In the present study, subjects reporting a recent history of NLS attempt participated in a semistructured interview aimed at reconstructing their state of mind in the hours leading up to their NLS attempt, with the goal of better understanding both situational factors and potential psychological pathways to suicide. Using a mixed methods design, a semistructured interview aimed at evaluating underlying themes of preoccupation, conflict, and defensive processes was developed and completed by participants along with self-report measures assessing demographic, psychiatric, suicide history, and mood. Participants (n = 11) were patients enrolled in residential treatment who had made an NLS attempt in the 2 years prior to participating in the study. The interviews were analyzed using Interpretative phenomenological analysis (IPA). Seven thematic units were identified as being associated with states of mind preceding a near-lethal suicide attempt. Results suggest that mental states and psychological processes preceding an NLS attempt are highly variable and embedded in longer-term developmental and characterological vulnerabilities. Participants reported efforts to conceal their imminent suicidal intent from their treatment providers and other sources of interpersonal support. States of mind preceding NLS attempts were found in this study to evolve in the context of developmental psychopathology, characterized by dimensional aspects that were multi-determined, interactive, and highly individual. Our findings lend support for approaches to treatment of suicidal individuals that emphasize understanding of highly specific elements of personal history and psychological vulnerabilities and the development of personalized interventions for suicide.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Unique Romance: Psychoanalysis and Phenomenological Philosophy: An Essay in Honor of Carlo Strenger

Frank Summers PHD ABPP

Frank Summers

333 E. Ontario Suite 4211B, Chicago, Illinois, United States. Email: franksumphd@gmail.com

Frank Summers  

phenomenology and existential philosophy, epistemology, therapeutic action, self-creation, individuality

This paper is inspired by the unique contributions of Dr. Carlo Strenger to psychoanalysis. Dr. Strenger was one of very few analysts who saw the foundation of psychoanalysis to lie in phenomenological philosophy. After a discussion of how Strenger used phenomenology to address and shed light on the epistemology of psychoanalytic psychology, the paper moves to the clinical arena. It is argued that Dr. Strenger developed a unique clinical approach that drew on the findings of existential and phenomenological philosophy. Building on this philosophical approach, Strenger shows that the mission of analysis, no matter the level of pathology of the patient, is to develop the patient's individuality. His clincial ideas were focused on helping the patient achieve this result. Dr. Strenger's creative analytic understanding of much cultural phenomena is discussed. Moreover and most importantly, by using this approach, he was able to bring to bear a unique perspective on clinical material in a fresh and compelling way. The result is a theory of therapeutic action that is sorely needed in our understanding of how the analytic process works.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bion's Alpha-Function as a Key for Understanding Emotional Problems in ADHD

Patricia Yudilevich MA and Edna BenEliahu MA

Edna BenEliahu

Email: pattyud@gmail.com
20 Mish'ol Ha'Ahava street, Kfar-Saba 4460120, Israel
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7870-5340

ednabe@arava.co.il
101 Barak Street, Moshav Pharan 8683500, Israel

Edna BenEliahu  

Bion, ADHD, alpha-function, children, regulation

This article discusses the attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) syndrome in children, from a regulation-based perspective, as a neurodevelopmental deficiency that develops into mental difficulties. Based on research indicating the existence of early markers to ADHD, we propose that the neurodevelopmental difficulty creates certain characteristics in the mother-infant/toddler relationship. We propose the term “mental sustaining” to describe the capacity to delay response in order to allow stimuli to be perceived and absorbed internally. In children with ADHD, the neurodevelopmental impairment of the regulatory function hinders the capacity for “mental sustaining,” potentially creating a vicious cycle in the child's relations with its caretakers. We draw on the thinking of Wilfred Bion, proposing that this vicious cycle might affect the quality of the internalization of alpha-function in a manner that results in various functional deficiencies. Three such deficiencies prominent in children diagnosed with ADHD are discussed in light of Bion's theory: the proclivity for idiosyncratic interpretations of reality; difficulty in automation of mental and motoric actions; and a tendency for excessive need for material gratification (the “bottomless pit” phenomenon). This article wishes to join the contemporary literature that understands children with ADHD from an inclusive theoretical perspective that links neurodevelopmental and psychoanalytic approaches. We propose a psychoanalytic understanding of this neurodevelopmental syndrome relying on Bion's concepts. The contribution of this article to existing psychoanalytic literature lies in its description and discussion of psychic mechanisms that are part of the characteristic psychic landscape of children with ADHD, as well as the presentation of potential etiologies of these mechanisms.

[[RunningHead]]

Oh No! I See a Pit: Making Sense of the Sensory on the Autism Spectrum

Nancy Crown PHD

Nancy Crown

350 Central Park West, Suite 2-H, New York, NY 10025, United States. Email: ncrownphd@macroweis.com

Nancy Crown  

autism spectrum disorder, sensory-perceptual processing, psychodynamic psychotherapy, deaf children with autism

With the rise in diagnoses of autism spectrum disorders over the past 25 years and the advent of improved and earlier intervention, more autistic children and adults are presenting for psychodynamic psychotherapy.1 This paper explores the unique sensory processing difficulties that are intrinsic to most individuals with autism, and thus central to their ways of being. While these challenges have long been recognized, their impact on the therapeutic process deserves particular attention, as this lens is crucial if we are to improve our understanding of and therapeutic utility with this population. This focus can also help therapists to manage common countertransference reactions and enable us to better support and engage family members of the autistic individuals we treat.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Autistic Disturbances in Skin Containment: The Dermic Drive as a Psychoanalytic Concept in the Study of Autism

Leon Brenner PHD

Leon Brenner

Berlin, Stromstraße 1, Berlin 10555, Germany
Email: leon@leonbrenner.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3028-5011

Leon Brenner  

autism, psychoanalysis, linguistics, skin, drive

Esther Bick is known as a pioneer in the elaboration of the psychoanalytic concept of the skin. Bick theorizes that the “primal skin function” is fundamental in the early formation and integration of the human psyche and suggests that it should determine most interpretations of psychopathology. This article investigates the particular association of disturbances in the skin function with the onset of autism. A new approach to the skin function is put in motion using terms borrowed from the mathematical field of topology developed by Bernard Burgoyne. Through this interpretation, autism is postulated as originating in disturbances in a particular modality of the skin function that cause psychic representations to operate according to a mode of separation characterizing specific topological spaces. This mathematical perspective explains the complexity and diversity of autistic symptoms in terms of linguistic functionality. Finally, the skin is put forward as a modality of the Freudian drive (Trieb)—the dermic drive—and the hypothesis of the psychic foreclosure of its rim is offered as a defining characteristic of the autistic psychic structure.

On Dreaming, Parasomnia, Dream Enactment, and Murder

Brent Willock PHD CPsych

Brent Willock

10 Avoca Avenue, Suite 301, Toronto, ON M5R 1A9, Canada. Email: bwillock@rogers.com

Brent Willock  

parasomnia, dreams, sleep disorders, violence, dream enactment

PEP Web's vast archive of psychoanalytic literature contains no articles on either parasomnia or dream enactment. There is no mention of violent parasomnias. Enriching dialogue is desirable between the study of these simultaneous sleep/wake states and the psychoanalytic investigation of dreams. This article illustrates how these fields of inquiry can be brought together to increase comprehension of significant phenomena. The concept of dream enactment bridges these disparate realms. A potential relationship between dreams of convenience and parasomnia is considered. These matters are insufficiently understood in mental health and legal disciplines, and in public discourse. Psychoanalytic perspectives on dreaming and dream enactment in sleep disorders have much to contribute to psychology, psychiatry, neuroscience, sleep medicine, and the law.

[[RunningHead]]

Qualities of Mentalization and Perception of Parental Mirroring in a Group of Italian Transgender People: An Empirical Study

Giovanna Esposito PHD , Cristiano Scandurra PHD , Maria Freda PHD , Giuliana Pepicelli , Paolo Valerio MD and Roberto Vitelli MD

Roberto Vitelli

Via Porta di Massa, 1, Naples 80133, Italy. Email: giovan.esposito@unina.it

Roberto Vitelli  

mentalization, transgender, mirroring, attachment, corrective practice

Transgender is an inclusive term referring to people whose gender identity, expression or behaviors are not fully aligned with those typically associated with their sex assigned at birth. Lemma has suggested that experiencing rejection in childhood might lead transgender people to feel an absence of mirroring by their parents in their early perception of gender incongruence and this might cause problems in mentalization. There have been no previous studies that have assessed mentalization (i.e., reflective functioning [RF]) in transgender people. In this study, we aim to assess RF in a group of transgender people and to relate RF to attachment patterns and perceived failures of mirroring. Twenty-eight adult attachment interviews (AAI), previously coded with regard to the attachment patterns, have been analyzed with the Reflective Functioning Scale [RFS]. A qualitative analysis of the AAIs has been performed to identify different qualities of perceived parental mirroring. The findings reveal that the majority of the sample presented a low RF (67.9%) and that three different qualities of perceived parental mirroring emerged (suppressing, avoiding, and pretending the gender incongruity), based on the “selectivity” of the mirroring process, one or both parents' mirroring abilities and the presence of significant others. The individuals with a secure attachment (50%), as well as those perceiving to have experienced a positive mirroring, showed a higher RF. This study indicates that both attachment patterns and mirroring experiences influence RF independently and that suppressing the gender incongruity may be considered the most pathogenetic category of mirroring in terms of RF development.

[[RunningHead]]

Keep It Quiet: Mother-Daughter Parentification and Difficulties in Separation-Individuation Shaping Daughters’ Authentic/True Self and Self-Silencing: A Mediation Model

Limor Goldner PHD , Chen Jakobi MA , Shirley Schorr MA , Serene Dakak MA and Nadeen Shawahne MA

Nadeen Shawahne

Aba-Hhusi 199 Street, Mount Carmel, Haifa 3498838, Israel. Email: limor.goldner@gmail.com

Nadeen Shawahne  

true self, authenticity, parentification, self-silencing, mother-daughter

The current study employed a mediation model to explore the contribution of mother-adolescent daughter (N = 208, Mage = 15.02) parentification and difficulties in separation-individuation to these adolescent girls’ authenticity/true self and self-silencing. Specifically, the model examined whether mother-daughter separation-individuation would mediate the relationships between mother-daughter parentification and motives for false-self behaviors and authenticity, which in turn would mediate the links between mother-daughter separation-individuation and self-silencing. The findings indicated that parentification was positively correlated with girls’ difficulties in separation-individuation, which in turn was negatively correlated with girls’ authenticity, and through it, was positively correlated with girls’ self-silencing. These findings highlight the negative consequences of parentification on girls’ self-system and point to the role of difficulties in separation-individuation and authenticity as mechanisms through which parentification contributes to Israeli adolescent girls’ self-silencing. These results are discussed in terms of attachment theory and developmental psychopathology.

[[RunningHead]]

Self-Creation and the Good Life: Carlo Strenger's Existential Psychoanalysis for Our Time

Danielle Knafo PHD

Danielle Knafo

10 Grace Avenue, Suite #7, Great Neck, NY 11021, United States. Email: danielleknafo@gmail.com

Danielle Knafo  

strenger, existentialism, self-creation, good life

This paper connects Carlo Strenger's life with his writings, how what he embodied as a life path is what he articulated in his work. Strenger left his Orthodox Jewish religion and home in Switzerland and recreated himself and his life in Israel. Drawn to the philosophy of existentialism, with its emphasis on the individual's irrefutable freedom and responsibility for the creation of meaning, Stenger stressed the creative possibility of reinvention throughout his career and utilized it in his work with patients, developing a unique and coherent psychology of self-creation that encouraged people to fashion a life grounded in their animating desires rather than one constrained by their past or the expectations of others. Here, I highlight the benefits and limitations of Strenger's theory and clinical approach.

[[RunningHead]]

Acting Out and Psychoanalytically Informed Treatment in Inpatient Adolescent Psychiatry

Carl Waitz PsyD

Carl Waitz

300 Longwood Avenue, Longwood IPS, Boston, MA 02115, United States
Email: Carl.Waitz@Childrens.harvard.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7701-5815

Carl Waitz  

Acting out, Lacanian psychoanalysis, adolescent psychoanalysis, behavioral health, inpatient psychiatry

The psychoanalytic concept of acting out has been largely reduced in the field of mental health to a byword for behaviors of which providers disapprove. This article seeks to revitalize the psychoanalytic concept of acting out by exploring its significance in work with adolescents in inpatient psychiatric settings who do not respond to behavioral health therapies. Utilizing a Freudian-Lacanian lens, this article examines the ways in which acting out is a response by the adolescent to an elimination of desire found within family systems as well as in psychiatric systems. The elimination of desire leads to the collapse of the fantasy that guarantees desire. Acting out, in the Freudian-Lacanian sense, is a method of reproducing that fantasy in action. By providing an interpretation or construction that touches upon the fantasy and the role of desire, such adolescents are able to reestablish the fantasy at the symbolic level and to cease their pattern of circulation through psychiatric systems. Two clinical vignettes provide examples of what such interpretations and constructions may look like in acute inpatient psychiatric settings. Clinical implications of this theoretical development will then be explored.
